data.title: PGIM Absolute Return Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

  • In Q1, U.S. rates and the dollar fell, and equities underperformed. Fixed income assets posted positive total returns even as spreads widened for most sectors. Spreads did tighten for European investment grade ('IG') corporates and certain securitized product subsectors (e.g., commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS'), collateralized loan obligations ('CLOs'), and asset-backed securities ('ABS'). Notably, European credit spreads tightened amidst expectations for accelerating European growth on the back of the announcement of a new fiscal package out of Germany.

  • Ongoing policy uncertainty seems destined to prompt bouts of market turbulence. In the U.S., anxiety regarding rapidly shifting government policies-e.g., Department of Government Efficiency ('DOGE') layoffs, tariffs, and the immigration crackdown-broke (or, at a minimum), interrupted the mystique of “American Exceptionalism.” The Federal Reserve (Fed) indicated that uncertainty may affect its future policy decisions, which adds context to potential market-related effects. That stated, when the Fed reaches the point of rate cuts, we believe it will act rather decisively in easing policy. Indeed, our market-implied model for three-month secured overnight financial rate ('SOFR') by year end indicates rising probabilities for a Fed funds rate between zero and 4.0% and falling probabilities for an outcome greater than 4.0%.

  • In addition, as U.S. policy uncertainty climbs, it appears poised to reorder global trade relationships. The U.S. administration’s imposition of reciprocal/across the board tariffs significantly increases the likelihood of tail outcomes on a global scale. Should trading partners retaliate with tariffs, we expect an even larger hit to growth.

  • Our assessment of the global macroeconomic fallout from the tariff developments (announced on April 2nd) includes a reduction in our 2025 U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) forecast from 2.2% to 1.5%. In a downside escalation scenario, that outcome could remove an additional one percentage point ('PPT') from the revised estimate, and the upside scenario of capitulation could add 0.5 ppt. We have also reduced our EA growth forecast from 0.8% to 0.6%, with the upside/downside scenarios carrying a 0.5 ppt adjustment in either direction. Our estimate for 4.2% GDP growth in China could take a 1 ppt hit in the downside scenario and receive a 1 ppt boost in the upside scenario.

  • Potential developments in our tariff-related base case scenario include: about half of the announced tariffs roll back on negotiations, leaving the effective U.S. tariff rate in the 12% area (up from the 2% area to start the year); less than a 1:1 retaliation from the rest of world (ROW); reduced potential for monetary and fiscal policy support vs. RoW amidst one-off price effects and possibly constrained fiscal space; gradual Fed rate cuts combined with relatively hawkish Fed rhetoric; and the likelihood that the European Central Bank (ECB) and Bank of England ('BOE') remain on their easing trajectories, with the ECB cutting the deposit rate two more times to 2% by the summer. Labor markets also remain key in this scenario with unemployment in the U.S. and UK remaining below 5% and below 7% in the EA.

  • Our downside scenario includes announced tariff levels that stick; expanded global retaliation that could encompass services; ECB/BoE rate cuts to below neutral, opening up fiscal space for industrial strategies/pacts across like-minded countries/regions; a slowing U.S. economy that increases the deficit further, potentially shrinking the capacity for fiscal stimulus; reduced inflows into the U.S. that push long-term yields higher and weaken the dollar; intensifying credit pressures; and a Fed that is compelled to address the fallout, abandoning its meeting-to-meeting, data dependent approach.

  • Our upside scenario is relatively capped (i.e., the asymmetric upside/downside changes to U.S. growth) amidst the lingering effects from uncertainty and strained relationships, and it potentially consists of another head fake where the U.S. and RoW capitulate to lower-than announced tariffs, leading to a rally in risk markets.

  • We would expect countries to safeguard against a “tariff cascade” wherein global excess capacity worsens and leads to dumping—a concern for large economic regions such as the European Union (EU)—that could add further downward inflationary pressure outside of the U.S. In such a case, the growth impact would be more significant than the inflationary impact. This moderating effect on global inflationary trends acts as an offset to trade barriers and is a key contributing factor to our overarching view of global moderation.

  • Meanwhile, China’s domination of global supply chains coupled with compositional differences in the trade basket and the underlying shift in Chinese consumption patterns suggest that retaliatory tariffs from China are unlikely to hurt Chinese consumers or change the deflationary narrative.

  • Investors are de-risking, and it is difficult to predict when this process will end. As current tariff policy presents a negative supply shock that may not reverse quickly. Furthermore, the Fed is in no rush to cut rates given inflationary risks. Policy uncertainty increases market pressure and financial stability risks. As a result, markets remain vulnerable even though valuations are cheaper.

  • In terms of credit, the healthy fundamental set-up at the start of the year has also been obscured by considerable trade uncertainty. Fundamentals still skew positive, but earnings expectations are coming down—albeit from healthy levels. We expect fundamental trends to vary by industry and remain watchful of more cyclical and consumer discretionary sectors, as well as those that might be disproportionately impacted by tariffs.

  • At present, we prefer to be patient and remain up-in-quality in risk markets. From current yield levels, bonds—especially those in the higher-yielding sectors—should generate respectable returns while intermittent volatility may continue to create opportunities to add value. That stated, extreme market movements are not to be confused with changes in fundamental market trends. As a result, we continue to rely on research and analysis to distinguish the market spasms from true changes in value and capitalize on reversions back to fundamentals.

1Q 2025 PERFORMANCE

  • The Fund (MUTF:PADZX) outperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index, on a gross basis.

  • Overall security selection was the largest contributor to performance during the period, with selection in U.S. Treasuries, emerging market ('EM') high yield, U.S. IG corporates, non-agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS'), non-agency CMBS AA-and-below, ABS, and British pound ('GBP') high yield corporates contributing the most.

  • Overall sector allocation also contributed to performance, with allocations to the U.S. IG corporate, EM high yield, GBP high yield corporate, and AAA CLO sectors contributing the most. This was partially offset by an allocation to the U.S. high yield corporate sector, which was the largest detractor during the period.

  • Within credit, positioning in telecom, contributed the most. Positioning in building materials & home construction, midstream energy, retailers & restaurants, and consumer non-cyclicals detracted the most.

  • During the quarter, the Fund’s yield curve and duration positioning contributed to performance as the yields on the intermediate- and long-end of the U.S. Treasury curve declined.

  • From a market perspective, having more risk, on average, over the period than the relevant benchmark detracted from results.

TOP-DOWN MACRO POSITIONING

Duration (Current: 0.2 yrs, Previous Quarter: 0.3 yrs)

  • At the outset of developed market central bank easing cycles, most anticipated a consistent, downward easing cycle that would take policy rates from their multi-year peaks to neutral (or thereabouts). However, the potential shifts in global trade, the relative stickiness of inflation, and overriding data dependency continues to reshape expectations.
  • The post-COVID bond market has been prone to dramatic overreactions and course corrections as participants struggle to understand the post-pandemic paradigm. Moreover, current geopolitical and U.S. policy dynamics have recently led to marked volatility (within the context of a bull market).
  • As a result, we have leaned long U.S. Treasuries and would reinforce that view, with more conviction at the belly given risks to growth and more cautious at the long end given macro/inflation/fiscal and term premium risks.

Curve Positioning

  • The moderating growth backdrop should support central banks’ pre-existing bias to cut rates and cushion downside risks, while making the possibility of rate hikes even more remote—despite the potential for tariff-driven increases in inflation.
  • Coming from a period of high inflation and nascent inflation expectations risks, the Fed has signaled a reactive approach going forward. While such an approach makes the Fed slow to react, it opens room for the market to price in lower rates on the front end/belly of the curve. However, lingering questions on debt dynamics and term premium may weigh on the back end.

BOTTOM-UP SECTOR POSITIONING

  • While the current economic environment remains favorable for U.S. and European corporate credit, the healthy set-up to start the year has been obscured by considerable trade uncertainty. Fundamentals still skew positive, but earnings expectations are coming down—albeit from healthy levels. We expect fundamental trends to vary by industry and remain watchful of more cyclical and consumer discretionary sectors, as well as those that might be disproportionately impacted by tariffs.
  • The upside/downside risk, from a market perspective, is lackluster as economic risks remain amidst elevated domestic and geopolitical uncertainty. We do not feel compelled to chase spreads in the current environment and favor carry, idiosyncratic stories, and relative value rather than directional views.
  • At this stage in the credit cycle, more frequent event risks, consumers’ increasing focus on essentials, and moderating credit metrics (along with increased distressed exchanges), point our allocations towards high-quality securities. We remain overweight an array of credit sectors including both investment-grade and high yield corporates, high-quality structured products, and EM.

CORPORATE CREDIT

Investment-Grade Corporates (Current: 7.2%, Previous Quarter 7.7%)

  • Credit fundamentals are somewhat weaker. Per Q4 2024 earnings releases, EBITDA margins were down 0.2 percentage points quarter over quarter (QoQ), and net leverage was flat (2.9x, QoQ), but up 0.1x year over year (YoY). Since then, Trump’s tariffs have exposed sectors such as autos, technology, and chemicals to additional risk. Considering policy uncertainty, Q1 earnings estimates have come down from an expected growth rate of +11.6% (YoY) at the end of 2024 to the current estimate of +7.1% (YoY).

  • Tariff policy announcements have eroded market confidence. The potential deferral of capex spending and moderation of mergers & acquisitions (M&A) activity in the upcoming quarters provide some reassurance, as it may help maintain corporate credit metrics. Furthermore, management teams may be reticent to reduce headcount considering the lack of macro clarity.

  • Despite the uncertainty, technicals (which have supported tight IG spreads have thus far) have largely remained in place with strong demand for high all-in yields. However, the relative attractiveness of U.S. IG has begun to lessen. A softer macro backdrop could bring lower yields, an influx of issuance, and a drop in demand which could lead to supply indigestion. On a positive note, our base case expectations are for net supply to fall as we enter the second quarter with less issuance and higher maturities than seen in the first quarter.

  • In terms of sectors, we are underweight industrials, and overweight financials (particularly the “Big 6” money center banks) and utilities. Potential market risks include an economic slowdown, consumer weakness, rate volatility, leveraging events like resurgent M&A or share buybacks, and geopolitical risks.

High Yield Corporates (Current: 11.7%, Previous Quarter 12.8%)

  • In high yield corporates, technical and credit conditions remain solid. The ongoing net supply deficit and robust inflows are supportive, and absolute yields remain attractive. Further, with overall levels of credit quality in the market at historic highs and a manageable near-term maturity wall, the default rate should remain benign. Furthermore, a significant dispersion in lower-tier credits could create relative value opportunities.

  • Although our base-case calls for moderating, yet continued, economic growth and contained inflation, trade conflicts and targeted Federal spending cuts create volatility and uncertainty around the path of economic growth. We have noted recent earnings weakness and weaker guidance in key industries, e.g., building products, and consumer products, however, we believe the central bank “put” remains in play should economic conditions deteriorate.

  • While the Trump administration’s agenda may seek long-term economic benefits, we are wary of the near-term effects. As such, we maintain our close-to-home defensive positioning with an overweight to high-quality, short duration high yield in the U.S. and underweights to cyclicals as well as potential tariff impacted names in Europe.

  • In terms of sectors, we are overweight home construction, healthcare & pharmaceutical, and midstream energy, and underweight technology, media & entertainment, and retailers & restaurants. Recent reductions have included cash positions trading at historically tight spreads in favor of high yield certificates of deposit (CDs) to capture the positive basis.

Bank Loans (Current: 1.2%, Previous Quarter 0.9%)

  • Although Q1 returns were muted, we continue to see another solid year ahead with a bank loan total return forecast of 6.5% in 2025. Our forecast is supported by high all-in current coupons and yields, strong CLO formation, and continued inflows from institutional and retail investors. However, sharply higher net issuance volume and easing SOFR rates bringing coupons lower as the year progresses could ultimately weigh on total returns.

  • We continue to favor public BB and high B loans over sponsor-owned, low B and CCC loans as we expect those lower-quality facilities will be challenged by the fundamental backdrop. We believe that credit selection and deep, fundamental credit research/modeling is becoming increasingly important. To that end, avoidance of defaults will likely be the biggest driver of alpha over the next 12-24 months.

SECURITIZED (ABS)

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities ('CMBS') (Current: 5.1%, Previous Quarter 4.3%)

  • Commercial real estate pricing continues adjusting to the elevated interest-rate environment, with price indices indicating an approaching trough in valuations. Delinquencies and modifications will continue as loans reach maturity or face refinancing challenges due to lower valuations and higher coupon rates.

  • We continue to see value within 5-year conduit AAA securities as spreads look attractive relative to similar tenor IG corporate bonds. Down the capital stack, we continue to see value in select single-asset, single-borrower securities and are selectively adding exposure.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOS) (Current: 22.0%, Previous Quarter 22.6%)

  • In CLOs, senior tranches continue to offer attractive relative value compared to many fixed income asset classes. However, credit concerns in the underlying bank loans could outweigh the prevailing technical support.

  • Furthermore, while bank loan spreads have been supported by low net supply and strong demand, we expect to see some credit deterioration via downgrades to CCC, increased default rates, and lower recovery rates.

  • We are selectively adding new issue mezzanine tranches where there are additional carry opportunities. In the U.S., we find value in selling higher premium, longer duration bonds, and rotating into spread-neutral primary transactions. In Europe, discounted bonds offer some total return potential.

Other ABS (Current: 2.2%, Previous Quarter 2.1%)

  • Prime consumer credit remains resilient, while the effects of inflation and lower disposable income continue to weigh on the weakest consumer segments and are starting to weigh on the near-prime segment. We remain vigilant for signs that the weakening in consumer credit is spreading. We remain positive on spreads in the near term but are mindful of heavy supply headwinds.

  • We favor top-tier unsecured consumer and subprime auto issuers, as well as significant risk transfers (SRTS) as they provide exposure to high-quality consumer assets. Looking forward, changes in banking regulation may lead to more opportunities via asset sales or regulatory transactions.

NON-U.S. BONDS

Foreign Government Related (Current: 0.2%, Previous Quarter 0.2%)

  • Some observers may regard Germany’s recent fiscal announcements as a sign that Europe is entering a new fiscal paradigm that is on a converging path with the U.S. This potential outcome is reflected in the thicker right tail of our European economic scenarios. The recent jump in 10-year bund yields to a near six-month high seemingly supports that right-tail narrative. However, with a base case for continued economic moderation, we view the market reaction as another case of an extreme market move under uncertain, volatile conditions.
  • Hence, long positioning in bunds—and other European rate markets where moves outpaced fundamentals—could be approached from a carry-and-roll perspective as well as from a relative value perspective vis-a-vis the U.S. Indeed, bunds appear to have more carry than U.S. Treasuries and are of higher quality with less outstanding supply and lower government deficits well into the foreseeable future. Furthermore, Europe’s latest inflation data remains in line with expectations, which is critical to facilitating our expectation of two additional ECB rate cuts to 2% by midyear.
  • Japan’s economy also followed a convergence theme in Q1 amidst its steady recovery and a solid Spring wage round. This should provide the Bank of Japan (BoJ) with room to gradually continue lifting policy rates. In divergence from the relatively flat move in the 10-year U.S. Treasury in Q1, the 10-year Japanese government bond yield continued its consistent climb into the 1.50% area.

Emerging Markets Debt ('EMD') (Current: 6.5%, Previous Quarter 7.6%)

  • Given the magnitude of the tariff news and the early reaction in markets, we do not yet think we are at an appropriate time to add risk. However, within many EM countries, we would take any meaningful sell-off as a promising buying opportunity as we believe we are entering a period when EM spreads and EM foreign exchange ('EMFX') can provide strong outperformance versus the U.S. and developed markets. Given the high all-in carry of the Index, we believe the uncertainty is being compensated fairly-the current coupon generation of the index can offset 95 basis points of spread widening without any move in core rates, so expected total returns skew positive.
  • EMFX—Currency dynamics are likely to be shaped by factors already identified as relevant to the wider EM asset class, including U.S. interest rates/Fed expectations. Potential performance of U.S. growth coupled with relatively high U.S. interest rates points to a small, long U.S. dollar bias against select low-carry currencies in Asia and Europe.
  • Following the tariffs announcement, we see the trajectory of the U.S. dollar as a two-part story. In the near term, the dollar could find additional support—as it did the day after the reciprocal tariff announcement on a safe-haven bid, Fed Chair Powell’s comments that it will be patient in setting monetary policy (not rushing towards rate cuts), reduced trade-related flows to the rest of the world, and entities that may accumulate dollars to repay dollar-denominated debt. Beyond these short-term effects, the dollar may be poised to continue its pre-April 2 weakening as the effective tariff rate acts as a similar devaluation on the currency. Eventual Fed rate cuts and the potential for equity market outflows could contribute to a weaker U.S. dollar over the long term as well.
  • EM Local Markets—We are cautiously optimistic on EM duration and expect a wide dispersion among countries due to idiosyncratic factors. However, a lack of support from further monetary easing amounts to a meaningful headwind.
  • EM corporates continue to offer opportunities to diversify, and we expect any spread weakness to be short-lived given the attractive yields and resilient fundamentals. The asset class has experienced fewer idiosyncratic surprises, and non-dedicated investors have shown appetite for the asset class given the spread pick-up. Near-term technicals could weaken somewhat on heavier supply and/or news of additional tariffs on China and Mexico.

U.S. GOVERNMENT RELATED

U.S. Treasuries (Current: 32.4%, Previous Quarter 27.0%)

  • At this point, the markets are pricing slightly more than 100 basis points of Fed rate cuts through year end. However, in addition to Powell’s reticence to quickly cutting rates, it’s unclear how much of a cushion they could provide the economy. That said, when the Fed reaches the point of rate cuts, we believe it will act rather decisively in easing policy. Indeed, our market-implied model for three-month SOFR by year end indicates rising probabilities for a Fed funds rate between zero and 4.0% and falling probabilities for an outcome greater than 4.0%.
  • The U.S. could also face mounting questions about its fiscal trajectory and the plans—or lack thereof—to address its expanding fiscal deficit. Treasury auction results will remain an area to watch closely, given the ongoing deterioration in U.S. fiscal measures.

U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities (Current: 7.4%, Previous Quarter 7.0%)

  • Although Index-level spreads are currently not overly compelling, zero-volatility spreads remain attractive - particularly in higher coupons.
  • Though we continue to expect net supply to look quite similar to 2024—less than $200 billion in total net issuance—given elevated mortgage rates and lack of affordable housing, we expect a pickup in origination in Q2 amid the sharp decline in rates in early April and as we enter the Spring upswing.
  • If the pace of rate cuts remains gradual, prepayment activity is likely to stay muted. Banks are also likely to remain a source of demand in 2025, with the potential that revised regulations under the Trump administration could render MBS holdings less capital-intensive.

Source: PGIM, Fund data as of 3/31/2025, unless otherwise noted.

Market overview

Tariff-related economic uncertainty is expected to slow consumer spending and business activity, a scenario that may bode well for collateralized asset-based lending (ABL) strategies. In a broader sense, these strategies have the potential to outperform unsecured corporate and consumer credit strategies in certain downside scenarios. Going forward, there continues to be an opportunity

to lean into attractive loans and leases backed by mission-critical assets.

Across categories, banks and traditional finance companies continue to retrench, leaving an opportunity set for non-bank asset-based lenders. The BASEL III end game has yet to be revised, but it will still be onerous for banking capital requirements. Banks and traditional finance companies have left a void in several ABL markets such as equipment finance and hard asset owners, and operators shifting to balance sheet light business models appear to be feeding demand for ABL and leasing strategies. Private ABL opportunities continue to be broad based, with new originations properly calibrated to new valuations and the economic reality.

Fund performance

Strong performance was observed across the residential mortgage, commercial real estate debt, aircraft consumer, and bank risk transfer components of the portfolio. Both credit selection and sector allocation continue to be major drivers of performance while geographic exposure has less of an effect, considering this is predominantly a U.S. based ABL strategy.

Strategy and outlook

Our investment landscape is evolving, driven by tight lending conditions due to higher capital charges and regulatory oversight. The focus remains on robust cash flows and asset security, particularly assets that are scarce in supply and high in demand. This guiding principle helps navigate an investment environment we believe to be marked by higher macro volatility, capital shortages, labor constraints, and selective availability of assets and equipment. Due to the collateralized nature and robust structure of the investments, recovery rates in ABL offer the potential for additional risk mitigation. We anticipate activity in the ABL market to remain elevated for the foreseeable future.

In this context, we believe the portfolio is well positioned, with the majority of capital now allocated to private ABL investments.

The strategy is to guide the portfolio toward an overweight in residential mortgages, aircraft, and equipment transactions, which hasn’t changed quarter over quarter. While we believe that some caution is warranted around certain unsecured consumer loan assets, we prefer to focus on asset-secured investments.

data.title: Oil Soars On Middle East Escalation
data.description:
By Warren Patterson

Israel strikes Iran

Israel has started conducting strikes against both Iranian nuclear facilities and military targets.

These attacks come at a time when the US and Iran are holding ongoing nuclear talks. Admittedly, there appeared to be little progress in these discussions recently, with both sides far apart. However, talks were set to continue this weekend. After these latest developments, it’s not clear if they will still go ahead. It is also worth mentioning that the US has stated that it has had no involvement in the strikes against Iran.

This is a significant escalation and differs from the strikes we saw last year, which spared Iranian nuclear sites. This will certainly lead to some form of retaliation from Iran against Israel. It will only lead to further uncertainty and increase the risk that regional energy supplies are disrupted due to the escalation.

While there are no reports of disruptions to oil supply, the market needs to start pricing in a larger risk premium.

How much oil supply is at risk?

Iran is a meaningful oil producer, pumping 3.3m b/d of crude oil and exporting in the neighbourhood of 1.7m b/d. In a scenario where we see further escalation, it’s not too difficult to envisage a situation where Iranian oil supplies are disrupted.

What impact this would have on the oil market depends on whether downstream assets are affected or if midstream and upstream assets are targeted. The latter would have more of an impact on the global market, putting as much as 1.7m b/d of export supply at risk. This would be enough to push the oil market from a surplus over the second half of this year into a deficit. It would prompt some fairly aggressive price revisions higher. This scenario could see Brent spiking to $80/bbl, although we believe prices will likely settle around $75/bbl. Much will depend on the supply response we see from other producers.

The Strait of Hormuz risk

In a scenario where we see continued escalation, there’s the potential for disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. This would impact oil flows from the Persian Gulf. Almost a third of global seaborne oil trade moves through the Strait of Hormuz. While some portion of oil flows could be diverted to avoid the Strait, it still leaves roughly 14m b/d of oil supply at risk. A significant disruption to these flows would be enough to push prices to $120/bbl. If disruptions persist towards the end of the year, we could see Brent trading to new record highs, surpassing the record high of close to $150/bbl in 2008.

Any disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz would also have a significant impact on the global LNG market. Qatar, which makes up around 20% of global LNG trade, uses this route to export LNG. Unfortunately, there is no alternative route. This would leave the global LNG market extremely tight, pushing European gas prices significantly higher.

How to make up for any potential supply shortfalls?

Any significant supply disruptions will push oil prices meaningfully higher. This would likely see governments around the world tapping into their strategic petroleum reserves. This would obviously have to be led by the US, which sits on more than 400m barrels of crude oil in its SPR.

The other solution would be OPEC tapping into its spare production capacity. OPEC has spare capacity of more than 5m b/d, and while they are in the process of bringing supply back on-line, a disruption to Iranian supply may prompt them to bring this supply back at an even quicker pace.

It’s important to note that while OPEC can provide a buffer to the market in the case of a loss in Iranian oil supply, it becomes more difficult if tensions spread. The bulk of spare production capacity sits in the Persian Gulf. So, if we are seeing disruptions to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, this spare production capacity will be of little help to the global oil market. Given the importance of the Strait, any disruptions would lead to a more globally coordinated response to ensure that energy flows at this chokepoint are not severely disrupted.

data.title: STRK: A Preferred Bet On Bitcoin, Yield, And Appreciation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Aaron Brown as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Recently, I’ve been thinking more about how to help set my toddler on a path toward financial independence when he reaches adulthood. So, when he won our family’s March Madness bracket pool, I used his winnings to seed a Uniform Transfers to Minors Act (UTMA) account. But I didn’t want his entire UTMA portfolio to consist solely of high-risk, high-reward bets. Instead, I was looking for exposure to an outperforming asset class with less volatility—and ideally, a steady yield that could serve as one of the foundational planks of his new portfolio.

Enter: Strategy’s (formerly MicroStrategy) convertible preferred stock - STRK (NASDAQ:STRK).

What Is STRK?

STRK is Strategy’s Series A Perpetual Strike Preferred Stock. It pays a fixed 8% annual dividend on its $100 par value ($2/quarter), which may be paid in cash, MSTR common stock, or a mix of both. While the dividend is perpetual, STRK currently trades above par, bringing the current effective yield closer to the mid-7% range. STRK also includes a conversion feature tied to Strategy’s common stock (MSTR) performance.

Think of STRK as a hybrid investment between a bond and a long-dated call option. It pays fixed dividend amounts and offers some downside protection through its liquidation preference, not dissimilar to a bond's interest income and return-of-capital features. But it also carries upside value via conversion rights tied to MSTR’s performance, like a long-dated call option that is currently out of the money. Holders can elect to convert during specific windows—quarter ends, redemption notices, or qualifying corporate events, which could make sense if the conversion would allow for appreciation under the 10:1 STRK to MSTR conversion ratio. Strategy may also redeem STRK under limited circumstances.

Until conversion or redemption, STRK sits above MSTR’s common stock and the recently announced STRD preferred shares in the capital stack but below STRF and its other debt. This structure of obligations is backed by 582,000 Bitcoins—over $62 billion at recent prices.

Why STRK Could Be An Opportunity

The market has recently begun recognizing that STRK (and one of Strategy’s other preferred share classes—STRF) may be mispriced. Over the last two months, STRK has traded from roughly $81 to over $107—a dramatic move for a preferred stock. There is no telling what the future price action will look like for STRK. During a significant drawdown, it is very possible that STRK could trade below its liquidation preference (its IPO price was $80/share, and I first bought shares for my son’s UTMA account at ~$85/share in April). But, even now, STRK’s yield still implies significant credit risk. Notwithstanding its large Bitcoin treasury, that disconnect likely reflects caution around its capital structure and lack of a credit rating—not its underlying solvency.

Looking to the future, it seems possible that STRK could appreciate materially because of yield compression and the embedded conversion value. Let me explain both.

Yield Compression. As with most fixed income instruments, broader market conditions could impact STRK’s yield. If market participants view STRK as a safer investment—perhaps due to newly issued credit ratings or reduced interest rates—the price of STRK could rise, driving the yield closer to the prevailing risk-free rate. One might argue that moving from $81 to $107 in a matter of a couple of months is the start of this compression (as the market’s perceived risk of STRK has lessened). But with a change in either Federal Reserve policy towards easing or newly issued ratings from a credit rating agency that recognizes Strategy’s large collateral base, such a move might become even more sustained. Of course, the inverse could also be true.

Conversion Value. Although STRK’s yield provides a steady income stream, at least part of its value also lies in its embedded conversion feature. This feature operates as a perpetual call option that allows holders to convert their shares into MSTR common stock at a 10:1 ratio. At the very least, the option to convert could provide a price floor once MSTR trades at a certain price (i.e., $1,000/share). MSTR is no stranger to parabolic moves because of its heavy exposure to Bitcoin (an inherently volatile asset), coupled with its large amounts of leverage. Even if MSTR doesn’t move to a four-figure amount per share in the short or medium term, the option can still add to the potential upside due to its perpetual nature. It is very telling that STRK and STRF have a similar market value on a per-share basis (currently STRK trades for around $2 more per share), even though STRK (i) is lower in the capital structure, and (ii) pays a 2% lower dividend on the par value. That implies that at least a portion of the delta in value (if not all) is connected to STRK’s unique conversion feature.

Put simply, STRK is not your average preferred stock. If Strategy’s Bitcoin treasury strategy succeeds and MSTR continues to appreciate over the coming years, it is plausible that long-term holders will get some equity-like upside after years of high-yield income collection. This arrangement provides the chance for upside that is otherwise virtually impossible to achieve with most fixed-income instruments. Because of the collateral and capital structure, this can be accomplished without taking the same downside risk of just buying MSTR.

Why STRK Could Be Risky

The original thrust underpinning the investment of STRK as a part of my son’s portfolio was STRK’s relative risk to MSTR. Time and time again, markets create opportunities when risk is misunderstood or mispriced. For STRK, its current yield (let alone its yield a month or two ago) implies substantial credit and structural uncertainty. This is the case even though Strategy’s current treasury dwarfs its total liabilities of ~$11.4B—including all debt and preferred equity—creating a current coverage ratio of around 6 to 1.

That high coverage ratio notwithstanding, no investment is without risk, especially one tethered to Bitcoin and an aggressive capital markets strategy. Here are three key risks I am thinking about:

Bitcoin Volatility. Although Strategy’s Bitcoin treasury is sizable, Bitcoin can also be volatile. In recent months, Bitcoin has traded from a low of $75,000 to a high of $112,000. It frequently experienced 75% to 80% drawdowns in prior bear markets from its previous all-time highs. Time will tell if the financialization of Bitcoin helps reduce the total peak-to-trough drawdowns. Still, there is no denying that such a violent downside move is possible and perhaps even likely at some point in the coming years. And with a violent downside move, Strategy’s collateral base would significantly reduce, which could impair its ability to raise new capital and cover its dividend payments under STRK and its other preferred securities, STRD and STRF.

Capital Structure Changes. Strategy retains discretion over its capital structure and the amount of debt and equity issuances it performs. So far, their capital markets strategy has been significantly accretive. But that doesn’t mean it will continue to be, especially in a bear market, and especially if Strategy becomes too leveraged. To Strategy’s credit, it aims to always have its total debt obligation under 30% of its net asset value (NAV). Still, the risk remains that the debt and preferred stock exposure could exceed 30% of its NAV due to a change in policy or during a drawdown. As debt gets closer to trading on par with NAV, the credit risk for STRK (and its ability to capture its full liquidation preference) could look dire. Additionally, the issuance of more preferred stock or debt on top of STRK, could also weaken its relative seniority.

Dividend Deferral & Reputational Risk. There is the risk that MSTR could elect not to pay a dividend under STRK. STRK is cumulative preferred stock, meaning missed dividends aren’t lost—they accumulate and must be paid before redemption or liquidation. But, missing a dividend (even though they accumulate) would likely spell disaster for STRK’s value and limit, or otherwise constrain, the robust access Strategy currently enjoys to the capital markets. Strategy will likely need to rely on the capital markets to support servicing its dividend payments, as it is not likely that operating cash flow alone would be sufficient to fund those obligations. Suspension of dividends is a reputational hit MSTR is unlikely to take lightly. Still, it might be the best choice for Strategy unless it wants to start selling its Bitcoin at cycle lows or diluting its common equity holders into oblivion.

Aside from the risks noted above, there are likely other risks (including potential phantom income tax exposure under the fast-pay stock rule, which could create tax headaches, especially in tax-advantaged accounts). In short, STRK isn’t risk-free, but is also not MSTR equity-level risk. STRK holders are higher in the capital stack and backed by tens of billions of dollars’ worth of the best-performing asset of the last decade and a half.

Conclusion

Strategy isn’t a traditional company. Its legacy operating business is modest (at least relative to its market capitalization), and its balance sheet is unapologetically volatile. But STRK lets you bet on Bitcoin with training wheels: a yield-generating vehicle that can ride to some of the upside without going headfirst into the downside. Or, put another way, with STRK you can collect dividends while waiting to see if Bitcoin (and by extension MSTR) breaks out to the highest reaches of some of the wildest price targets from the industry. STRK isn’t just a high-yield income product. It’s also a perpetual call option on Bitcoin’s success, delivered in a wrapper that offers income, relative downside protection, and potential capital appreciation. This can be particularly powerful if done tax-efficiently through a tax-advantaged account, or, in my son’s case, by utilizing his UTMA to receive tax-free treatment for STRK dividends under the kiddie-tax threshold.

STRK may not be the right product to include in every portfolio. But for those seeking an investment that blends income stability with the potential for high-reward growth, STRK is worth a look. Currently, it is one of my preferred approaches to Bitcoin exposure.

data.title: John Hancock Municipal Opportunities Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • A record pace of new municipal bond issuance and uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade and tariff policy kept municipal bonds in check during the first quarter, trailing the positive returns in the broader bond market.
  • The fund modestly outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index.
  • A noteworthy position in high-yield municipal bonds and a beneficial maturity structure contributed the most to the fund’s outperformance.

Market review and outlook

Municipal bonds posted mixed results in the first quarter and underperformed the broader bond market. One factor was the record pace of new issuance during the period on the heels of an all-time high in municipal bond issuance in 2024. Although signs of slowing economic growth were supportive for bonds in general, uncertainty about U.S. trade and tariff policy led to a risk-off market environment in the latter half of the quarter. As a result, investors flocked to U.S. Treasury securities and other safe haven investments. In this environment, investment-grade municipal bonds declined slightly, while high-yield municipal bonds produced modestly positive returns.

We expect volatility in the municipal bond market to remain elevated in the coming months amid continued U.S. economic and trade policy uncertainty coupled with a continuation of the robust new issuance of municipal bonds we’d seen over the past year and a half. Nonetheless, we continue to believe that 2025 will be a coupon-clipping year, meaning that interest income is likely to make up most, if not all, of the overall return for municipal bonds. Within the fund, we continue to focus on adding incremental yield while maintaining a notable position in high-yield municipal bonds.

Contributors and detractors

The fund narrowly outperformed its benchmark index, posting a fractional decline for the quarter. The fund’s yield curve positioning was the key factor behind its outperformance of the index, particularly an overweight position in the intermediate maturity sector and an underweight position at the long end of the yield curve. The fund’s holdings of high-yield municipal bonds also aided performance as these securities outperformed during the quarter.

Individual security selection generated mixed results in the first quarter, ultimately detracting slightly from performance. The fund benefited from selection among healthcare bonds, particularly our emphasis on bonds funding regional hospitals, but this was offset by security selection among industrial development bonds. Pricing differences also dented performance slightly.

Portfolio changes

We lowered the fund’s weighting in healthcare bonds during the quarter, in part to take profits following strong gains in 2024, and in part due to uncertainty about potential cuts to Medicare and Medicaid. We redeployed these assets into the transportation sector with an emphasis on airports, where yields are especially attractive.

data.title: TEC:CA ETF: The Only True Tech ETF In Canadian Dollars
data.description:
What is TEC:CA?

The TD Global Technology Leaders Index ETF (TSX:TEC:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of ~$4Bn CAD that invests in globally listed technology stocks. While there are some other ETFs that have higher technology exposure in Canada, TEC:CA is the only comprehensive option to get a broad-based technology ETF with low fees - and think it's the best representative of the total technology market. TEC:CA is very liquid, and this article seeks to compare this offering with some other larger passively managed fund offerings, notably First Trust AlphaDEX™ U.S. Technology Sector Index ETF (FHQ:CA) and iShares S&P/TSX Capped Information Technology ETF (XIT:CA). While there are many actively managed fund managers who have higher fees and smaller portfolios, I'm reviewing this passive tech equity ETF against two well-known peers and the market given the abundance of funds in Canada, but lack of liquidity in many offerings.

TEC:CA's benchmark target is the Solactive Global Technology Leaders Index and after further review, I believe that TEC:CA is worthy of the title as top technology focused ETF in Canada. The fund has closely followed its benchmark, while outperforming other passively managed tech ETF's, and sporting low fees with lots of liquidity. If investors want to get exposure to global technology equities using Canadian dollars in a fund that truly represents the sector, and is not a rehash of a basic index using a different brand/literature, TEC:CA is a solid choice.

Fund Breakdown

TEC:CA was launched on May 7, 2019, by TD Asset Management with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that tracks the performance of a global equity index which measures the investment return of global mid- and large-capitalization issuers related to technology. The ETF has successfully tracked the total U.S. market for over several years now, and has been a clean way to get a mix of large and mid-cap exposure tech.

Additionally, the fund has lots of liquidity, with daily volumes averaging over 200,000 shares traded, which equates to ~$9MM CAD of volume per day. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing the ETF units against the underlying holdings. Overall, the structure of the fund is solid and Canadian investors have been receptive to buying it to get tech exposure.

It's important to note that the fund is not currency hedged. TD has another fund, TECX:CA, which is hedged, and this is important because most of the fund's investments (~87%) are American. Currency hedging costs and performance drag can play a role in underperformance, as CAD-hedged ETFs can use some derivatives to eliminate USD/CAD exchange rate fluctuations. Over time, hedging costs and inefficiencies can drag down returns. When the USD appreciates against CAD, unhedged ETFs benefit because their U.S. holdings become more valuable in Canadian dollars. Recently, CAD/USD has strengthened towards 1.35 after a rocky start to the year, so the fund has not fully taken advantaged. Hedged ETFs don't receive this benefit since they neutralize currency movement, missing out on gains when CAD depreciates. I prefer the unhedged option as while Trump is chaotic, I simply don't see the muscle being applied in Canada to drive new innovation and productivity to such an amount that it's currency rallies significantly.

The fund has done well against peers over the past 4 years. While XIT:CA focuses on Canadian technology, the sector is not as robust, and a huge part of the fund holds Shopify (SHOP:CA). Meanwhile, FHQ:CA is another good option, but it's focused only on U.S. holdings and smaller, high-growth companies, leading to lower weightings in traditional tech-heavy weights like AAPL and MSFT. I don't mind it, but having a core holding in these global giants that impact billions of lives is probably a good bet, and more importantly, more easily recognizable for investors who want to invest in a tech ETF.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing TEC:CA's portfolio, there are some important differences in the portfolio breakdown to its peers. As of Feb. 28, 2025, the portfolio resembles its index, with top holdings like AAPL, MSFT and NVDA, while FHQ:CA holds stocks like PLTR. I also think TEC:CA's fund's expense ratio of 0.39% is reasonable, given it's the only high-liquid Canadian dollar ETF that truly tracks the tech industry.

TEC:CA has a Sharpe ratio of 1, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, though around 1 is good as an investment. As expected for a fund in technology, the beta is over 1, at 1.35 and that is acceptable for the profile of the fund. Ultimately, its risk-adjusted returns are strong, and the fund has continued to beat peers in Canadian technology, while holding the right global mix to counter FHQ:CA's U.S. mid-cap tech growth focus.

Risks & Bottom Line

The key risk in investing in this fund is that the global economy slides, which would make the investment depreciate and effect both large and smaller companies. Recently, the U.S. budget has been in flux, and I can't predict what will happen next in regards to the market. The fund holds large cap tech stocks, and if the market crashes, you'd have nowhere to hide here. But the bottom line is that this fund is an easy way to get tech equity exposure in Canada. The fund has a reasonable expense ratio that is comparable to peers and the fund is very liquid. If investors want tech investments in Canada, TEC:CA makes a lot of sense.

data.title: John Hancock Multi-Asset Absolute Return Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Global equity markets declined in the first quarter amid economic uncertainty brought on by changing U.S. trade and tariff policies, leading to a significant shift toward risk aversion.
  • Global fixed-income markets were mixed but generally positive as expectations for slowing global growth led to lower bond yields during the quarter.
  • The fund posted a positive return for the quarter and outperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA 0–3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index.

Market review and outlook

Global financial markets faced significant economic uncertainty in the first quarter. U.S. tariffs targeting all trade partners and most economic sectors raised inflationary fears and growth concerns. This backdrop led the U.S. Federal Reserve to pause its series of rate cuts, citing the need for greater clarity on economic conditions. In this environment, stock markets in developed countries declined, due largely to a drop in the U.S. market. In contrast, emerging-market stocks posted positive returns. Performance was also divergent in fixed-income markets, with the U.S. market enjoying a rally amid lowered economic expectations, while European bond markets fell in response to expansionary fiscal policies. Risk-off market sentiment led to the outperformance of investment-grade securities over high-yield bonds during the quarter.

In our view, U.S. tariff policies pose a growing downside risk to the global

economy. The economic consequences depend on what else is in store—how long the tariffs will be in place, whether there will be further modifications, and how the revenue from the tariffs will be used. In the near term, we’ve already seen worrying signs in consumer sentiment and uncertainty indicators, and companies may take a wait-and-see approach to capital decisions, which could further weigh on economic activity. Meanwhile, changing geopolitical circumstances add to the complexity and uncertainty. In challenging times, the value of a risk-managed strategy within a diversified investment portfolio can’t be underestimated.

Contributors and detractors

The fund posted a positive overall return in the first quarter. The defensive strategy in developed equity markets contributed the most to performance, benefiting from the fund’s short positions and low-risk value component. Fixed- income holdings, concentrated in government bonds and mortgage-backed securities, also added value.

On the downside, the fund’s equity holdings in developed markets weighed on performance as developed stock markets declined broadly. The fund’s currency management strategy was also a drag on results, due primarily to the U.S. dollar’s weakness relative to most foreign currencies.

Portfolio changes

The fund’s net equity exposure declined in the first quarter, reflecting the generally negative environment for stocks. Meanwhile, its interest-rate sensitivity increased amid expectations for further central bank interest rate cuts. We continued to avoid exposure to corporate credit.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

data.title: International Seaways: Sailing With Fundamental Resilience But Upside Is Limited
data.description:
With the current volatile market conditions and weak oil prices, International Seaways, Inc. (NYSE:INSW) faces some challenges today. Pricing volatility and softer demand are evident considering its most recent performance. That is why I believe its recent price correction was warranted, although its price today is still quite high for its valuation. Technicals show some bullish signals, but more confirmations are needed.

Nonetheless, the company demonstrates resilience with its healthy Balance Sheet and young vessels age.

INSW Q1 2025

International Seaways, Inc. remained weak at the start of the year as softer market conditions continued to affect its pricing power and demand. Its Q1 2025 revenue took a nosedive amid its weaker performance. It amounted to $183.4M, down by -33.2% YoY from $274.4M. It is also important to note that revenue dropped for five consecutive quarters. It was also the sixth quarter where INSW had a YoY revenue drop, the largest drop to be more specific.

We can attribute it to pool revenues, which dropped by -40% YoY. Continued oil price decline remained a primary cause as it lowered the revenue and profitability of oil explorers and producers. The lower the incentives they get from producing oil, the lower their production is to stabilize expenses and margins. As a result, their demand for oil and petroleum transporters like INSW. At the end of Q1 2025, Brent and WTI prices per barrel were $74.7 and $70.3, respectively. They were about 20% lower than their value on the same day in the previous year.

This even affected spot rates, which were crucial for INSW's pricing and revenue. For instance, the spot rates of its VLCC dropped from $44,736 to $33,531. I also think that its heavy reliance on spot rates became a challenge. Its huge exposure to the spot market made it vulnerable to volatility risks. As we can see, over 80% of its revenue days came from spot rates. So, a weak market condition can easily weaken spot rates.

With respect to profitability, INSW also had a hard time managing its costs and expenses. It even became more cost-sensitive as its operating leverage rose from 60.6% to 65.3%. On a lighter note, we cannot deny that INSW maintained a high-margin business despite the sticky inflation. While it's not good to see higher prices despite lower revenue days, revenues remained adequate to cover it. As a result, its operating margin dropped from 56.2% to 32.2%. Yet, it was still high to cover other expenses and taxes. Hence, the company remained profitable.

Challenges

Considering the current market conditions, heavy exposure to the spot market can become a disadvantage to INSW. As I've explained, spot rates are highly sensitive to market volatility, particularly oil prices and demand. Oil prices are weak, and so are spot rates.

The challenge may persist in the second half as Brent and WTI remain weak compared to 2024. This trend may continue as the OPEC intends to make another hike this July.

Yet, we must not easily conclude about it. Note that tensions in the Middle East are still on the rise. Note that Brent and WTI rose by 4.3% to $69.77 and 4.9% to $68.15 yesterday. They reached their highest price since April. Now, the question lies in the opposing impact of the OPEC oil hike versus the Israel-Hamas War.

On a lighter note, the potential advantage of the continued oil price drop will lower fuel expenses in the long run. This can also temper the impact of tariffs, which can indirectly affect oil prices and demand.

Drivers

At this point, you may wish to immediately avoid this stock. But I advise you to think twice, since the company remains profitable despite the challenges it faces today.

But what's more interesting is its strong Balance Sheet that promises sustainability of its operations and dividends. Its cash has remained adequate and quite stable at over $100M in the past 11 quarters. This can cover all liabilities, excluding capital leases. Cash alone can pay all borrowings at once, even INSW does not have to. Note that the total borrowings have already dropped from about $560M to only $60M, making it very liquid today. Even without using cash, the company can easily cover it, as Debt/EBITDA is only 0.15x. Hence, it only needs two months to pay all borrowings. It has enough capital buffer and earnings to sustain its operations.

Moreover, INSW maintains a decent vessel age to ensure sustainability and efficiency in the long run. There are MR vessels aged about 17 years, but they are still below the average efficient life of 25 years. In addition, it now has 3 VLCCs, which are only about two years old. It also ended the quarter by swapping its oldest VLCCs for three 2015-built MRs. This younger age of the fleet may improve its performance in the long run. They can promise more efficient fuel consumption and fewer needs for repairs or maintenance.

Lastly, it also slightly increased its exposure to fixed TCE rates from 16% to 19%. While it is still quite insignificant relative to spot rates, this is a small step to strategically balance its volatility risk and earning exposure to stabilize revenue and margins. Of course, it will take time for this goal to materialize.

Valuation

INSW corrected after its 2024 rally. It weakened some more in March and April. I think that's warranted if we relate it to its key metrics, such as sales per share or SPS and BVPS.

If we look at its P/B in 2024, it became unusually high at 1.33x when historically it was less than 1.00x. Today, it appears fairly valued at a P/B of 1.00x. However, it is still higher than the five-year average of 0.93x. This makes its valuation quite expensive relative to previous years. If we use the average P/B to derive its fair value, the TP will be $35.08. We can observe the same thing in the P/S Ratio. It is already much cheaper than in 2024. Yet, it is still more expensive than the historical average. This derived a $33.67 TP. Both price ratios suggest that the stock is still quite expensive. The consolation is that it is already near the fair value, so we must start keeping an eye on this stock.

Technicals

INSW's momentum has already improved after its correction. The price is now increasing and moving above the 50-day SMA. We can now see some bullish signals, supported by the recent shift in the direction of the 50-day SMA. Despite this, I advise investors to remain cautious to avoid a bull trap. Note that the 200-day SMA has not yet flattened and is still above the price and the 50-day SMA. Although SMAs are lagging indicators, it is essential to wait for more bullish confirmations.

MACD and RSI support our cautious optimism, as they have been showing weaker momentum in the past two weeks. Somehow, I understand the behavior since the stock has become overbought recently. Profit takings and lower buying pressures prompted the dip as the market tested the $40 resistance. On a lighter note, its RSI shows room for taking a buy position again.

Lastly, we may refer to the Fibonacci Retracement. We can see that the rebound stopped temporarily when the price got very close to the 0.382 Retracement point. It was an indication of resistance testing. The momentum weakened again, although it did not go back to the 0.236 level. This agrees with the early bullish signals by the price and the 50-day SMA. But at this level, we can also see a consolidation. If the price crosses above 0.382 Fibonacci, it will break above the resistance and may lead to a breakout. If not, it may go back to the 0.236 mark. This will be close to my P/B TP. Hence, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines for now to avoid a bull trap.

Conclusion

International Seaways, Inc. remains strong despite market headwinds. Its maintained profitability and solid Balance Sheet show its sustainability. Yet, I advise investors to stay cautious as its valuation suggests a limited upside potential. Technicals are improving, but more confirmations are needed to avoid a bull trap. I recommend a hold rating for now as we wait for more bullish confirmations.

data.title: What Cooling U.S. Inflation Could Mean For The Bond Market
data.description:
Despite tariff pressures, U.S. inflation cooled in May. But is it too early to declare the all clear? Hafiz Noordin, Vice President & Director, Active Fixed Income, TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss how the bond market is viewing global trade tensions and the potential impact on inflation.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Despite tariff pressures and concerns, US inflation actually cooled in May. But is it still too early to declare an all-clear? Joining us now to discuss what all this means for interest rates in the bond market is Hafiz Noordin, VP and Director Active Fixed Income At TD Asset Management.

Hafiz, welcome back to the program.

Hafiz Noordin: It's great to be back.

Greg Bonnell: Interesting-- we've been watching the data points so closely to try to figure out with all this trade turmoil, with all this tariff uncertainty, when is it going to show up in inflation? We have fear of inflationary pressures. We're not seeing them. What's going on?

Hafiz Noordin: Yeah. So, obviously, we know that the effective tariff rate has gone up despite a walk back of the extreme tariff talk that the Trump administration had. So there was definitely a lot of attention on, particularly, goods prices within the inflation data to see if it was ticking up. And we didn't get that.

So the market was expecting headline inflation of 2.4% year-over-year. It came in-line on a headline basis. But on a core inflation basis, the market was expecting 2.9%. It actually only came in at 2.8%-- so a little bit lighter. And on a month-over-month basis, the miss looks actually larger.

The market was expecting 0.3% month-over-month inflation in May, and it came in at 0.1% month-over-month on a core inflation basis. So, absolutely, it seems like it's more of the growth slowdown theme that's been happening this year in the US that's been feeding into the inflation data a little bit more recently. And the tariff impact is still yet to show up in inflation. But I think that will come over the next several months.

Greg Bonnell: Alright. Let's go there, yet to show up. Because I've seen a lot of commentary around it basically just saying, well, some people stockpiled ahead of time to get their goods they have in front of their customers. They don't want to raise prices on customers because of the sensitivity around the cost of living. It all seemed to add up to me that, yeah, it was fine to say this for the month of May, but we got to start looking in the months forward and trying to figure out where we're headed. Where could we be headed?

Hafiz Noordin: Yeah. And, to a certain extent, the market's already priced some of this in. The thinking is that, yeah, up to now, there's been a lot of front-loading of purchasing. So there's a lot of inventory sitting out there that is kind of pre-tariff inventory, so it doesn't reflect higher prices yet.

But when the market's been looking through the numbers, it's pricing in inflation on a core basis, and particularly using core PCE, which is the one that the Fed looks at when thinking through policymaking-- core PCE to go up and peak at around somewhere between 3% to 3.5% by Q3 of this year. And so, that's what's already baked into the market.

And I think over the next several months, everyone will be watching to see, are the numbers truly trending in that direction? Or are they a little bit worse or better? So, I think there's already a certain amount of expectation that, yes, goods prices will be going up a bit. But I think the Fed is looking through all that. So inflation expectations longer term still haven't been really impacted a lot by tariffs.

Greg Bonnell: The Fed's obviously under political pressure to cut rates, and perhaps by a sizable amount. The Fed has basically said we need to wait and see how this plays out in the economy. When you get inflation reports like this, how difficult does the Fed's job get when it seems like we're still above target, but we're not having these pressures? How hard is it for them to stay the course?

Hafiz Noordin: It is difficult in that we know from the post-COVID experience that what may seem like a rise in prices that seems temporary-- that sort of dreaded word, transitory--

Greg Bonnell: Transitory-- not transitory.

Hafiz Noordin: They're going to make sure not to use that word again, right? But we know that there's always some amount of uncertainty of whether the economy and households view an increase in prices as permanent or not, and whether that impacts their own inflation expectations. And that's very difficult to measure.

And so, I think that's the part where, having learned from that mistake a few years ago, they're going to tread carefully. And that's why, while many developed markets central banks have cut towards basically what they would call neutral, the Fed is still sitting out there, only having cut about 100 basis points.

They're still at a 4.5% policy rate, which is well above their estimate of neutral. And so I think that's where the tension is there that, you have the Trump administration calling for at least getting back to neutral.

But with inflation-- and, as I mentioned, core inflation still expected to rebound to 3% to 3.5%-- it's hard for them, as an inflation-targeting central bank, to necessarily cut aggressively. So the data will have to really guide the path for them.

Greg Bonnell: That's what the Fed is trying to make of all this. What's the bond market making of all this?

Hafiz Noordin: Well, today certainly, the bond market reaction is pretty positive. You have about a 6-basis point decline in 2-year yields. That basically reflects a modest increase in the probability that the Fed can cut later this year, start getting back towards neutral.

But when you step back and look at what's happened year-to-date with bond yields, 2-year yields are down about 25 basis points from the beginning of the year, again, reflecting a bit of this lower growth in the US. But 10-year yields started the year about 4.5%. When all is said and done, we're still about 4.5%.

And longer-dated bond yields are actually up a little bit, about 20 basis points. So, really, this environment we're in is that a slowdown in growth is expected, inflation expected to come up a little bit. But it's not translating into aggressive rate cut expectations quite yet.

And so I think the good thing, though, for the market, and for the equity market, too, is that at least bond market volatility has come down a lot from tariff talk being walked back. And the fact that inflation is at least behaving right now is helping to maintain bond volatility at these more subdued levels.

Greg Bonnell: If we want to bring it back to Canada-- obviously, we have our own economy, we have our own issues. But I've often heard over my career of doing this finance stuff that we import bond yields. The American situation, how much does it press on our situation?

Hafiz Noordin: Yeah. So, the movement in bond yields this year has been fairly similar. I mentioned the 10-year in the US, where we started-- Canada, similarly, about 3.2%, 3.3% is where we'd started at the beginning of the year, and that's where we are today.

I think the main difference, though, in where we think of Canadian bond yields going forward, is that the Bank of Canada has already cut about 225 basis points from their peak, while the Fed's only cut about 100 basis points. That really defines the big difference in shorter-dated bond yields, where the market is pricing in that the Bank of Canada has already kind of close to its ending point in terms of bringing policy rates back to normal.

And so, I think that's what really will define the outlook going forward is, we may see Canada bond yields a little bit more stable in the front end, while US bond yields may have some room to come down more if inflation continues to behave. And so, I think that's really the key difference between the two economies. But there's still always going to be some amount of correlation, I think, between bond yields in US and Canada.

Greg Bonnell: Well, take Canada against the US-- does that mean now that there's more opportunity in US fixed income as opposed to Canadian?

Hafiz Noordin: So, it's a great question because, at a headline level, we see these higher yields there, right-- 4.5% US 10-year versus 3.2%, 3.3% in Canada. But one of the biggest things that have changed, because of this divergence in monetary policy between the two economies over the last year, the cost of hedging the US dollar back to the Canadian dollar has increased substantially. It's about 1.5% per year in terms of your cost in terms of yield.

So that 4.5% US tenure, after you hedge it back to Canada, on a yearly basis, is more of a yield of around 3%. So you actually still have a higher yield in Canada after factoring hedging costs. And so I think, as investors we have to keep that in mind that it's not just about the headline yield, but currency volatility has been a big theme. And that translates into real hedging costs. So for now it still means that I think Canada, and particularly shorter-dated Canada bond yields, are a good place in terms of low volatility and pretty decent income.

data.title: NIO: Impact Of New Pricing War In China
data.description:
NIO’s (NYSE:NIO)(OTCPK:NIOIF) recent earnings miss has not resulted in a big correction in the stock. One of the reasons could be that the negatives have been priced in. NIO missed EPS estimates by $0.05 and revenue estimate by $56 million. The company showed an improvement in vehicle margin to 10.2% in the recent quarter from 9.2% in Q1 2024. The gross margin has increased to 7.6% from 4.9% in Q1 2024. The R&D and marketing costs are still quite high, and we could see some efficiency in these expenses in the next few quarters. The last few quarters were very tough for the EV market in China, with massive price cuts by market leader BYD. In late May, BYD announced another major round of price cuts, which has alarmed many industry leaders and regulators. Despite these price cuts, NIO has been able to show YoY improvement in margins. In the previous article, it was mentioned that trade war could be a tailwind for NIO.

For the second quarter, NIO is projecting 25.5% to 30.7% YoY growth in vehicle deliveries. Its new ONVO brand is showing a good growth trend with a more affordable price range. The forward P/S ratio of NIO is only 0.69 for the current fiscal year ending Dec 2025 and 0.53 for next fiscal year. This is despite the massive revenue growth projection. If the margin improvement continues in the next few quarters, we could see a much better bullish sentiment towards the stock.

NIO’s earnings miss does not cause a correction

NIO missed both EPS and revenue estimates in the recent earnings. The revenue miss was $56.14 million, which is almost 3% of the actual quarterly revenue of $1.67 billion. However, this disappointing number did not lead to a correction in the stock in the last few days. One key reason is that most of the headwinds have likely been priced in by Wall Street.

Figure: EPS and revenue miss by NIO in the recent quarter. Source: Seeking Alpha

Compared to other EV stocks in China, NIO has already seen a dip in YTD. This has changed the valuation dynamics for NIO stock which is now trading at a significant discount to many other peers when we look at the P/S ratio. It is also important to note that NIO has been able to show YoY margin improvement despite the intense pricing pressure of the last few quarters.

Future of margins amid pricing cuts by BYD

NIO’s vehicle margin in the recent quarter was 10.2% compared to 9.2% in Q1 2024. This is a 100 basis points improvement in a key metric. There has been QoQ decline. In Q4 2024, NIO had a vehicle margin of 13.1%. Hence, there has been almost 2.9 percentage point dip when we look at QoQ. The sales of the affordable ONVO brand in also increasing, which might put some pressure on the margins. However, it is still possible that NIO could deliver better vehicle margins in the next few quarters as it scales up the deliveries. The gross margin has increased to 7.6% in the recent quarter from 4.9% in the year-ago quarter.

Figure: Vehicle and gross margins of NIO in the recent quarter. Source: Company Filings

Recently, BYD has announced another major round of price cuts, which has reduced the prices as much as 34% on some models. BYD’s affordable Seagull hatchback is now sold for $7,700 compared to $10,000 earlier. It is possible that regulators in China might step in to prevent a “race to the bottom”. China’s official Communist Party paper, the People’s Daily, mentioned that this price war is unhealthy and hurts the entire ecosystem.

The jury is still out on how this price war ends. However, it is possible that we might be close to the bottom in terms of pricing as regulators push all industry players in China to maintain a sustainable ecosystem. China’s real estate has already been struggling due to intense competition among players, which ended up causing headwinds for the overall economy. The regulators might want to prevent a similar situation in the EV segment, where intense competition hurts the profit margins for all players and also ends up hurting the wage growth within this industry.

Good delivery projection for second quarter

NIO has projected deliveries between 72,000 and 75,000 in the second quarter. This would equate to 25.5% to 30.7% growth compared to the year-ago quarter. The delivery growth momentum for NIO is quite good, and we could see a better trajectory as the sales of the more affordable ONVO brand increases in the next few quarters.

Figure: Forward delivery projection of NIO. Source: Company Filings

NIO had earlier mentioned that it would like to increase exports. It wants to sell a new hybrid model from 2026 only in overseas market. This could help increase the margins for the company, especially in lucrative markets like Europe.

Risks and modest valuation

NIO does face an uphill task in maintaining its market share, as intense competition in China shows no sign of easing. The latest price cuts by BYD are a clear signal that we could see a few more quarters of pricing pressure. Unless we see some strong moves by the regulators, NIO could show some headwinds in the near term. Another negative factor for NIO is the high R&D and marketing expense. The SG&A expense increased from almost 3 billion RMB in Q1 2024 to 4.4 billion RMB in Q1 2025. This is almost 46% YoY growth compared to 20% growth in revenue. The scaling up of ONVO brand sales might have led to a higher expense. But it is very important for the management to show better control over this expense in the next few quarters.

Figure: Increase in expenses. Source: Source: Company Filings

Despite the risks, the modest valuation shows that NIO can still deliver good returns. The forward P/S ratio for fiscal year ending Dec 2025 is only 0.69 and 0.53 for fiscal year ending Dec 2026. The consensus YoY revenue growth for this year is 36% and 31% for next year. However, it should be noted that there is a massive gap in low and high revenue estimates. This shows the risk associated with the stock and the uncertainty to deliver the vehicle sales projected.

Investor Takeaway

NIO missed the estimates in the recent earnings. However, it is showing YoY improvement in vehicle and gross margins. The forward delivery estimates are also quite strong. NIO has been able to weather pricing cuts in 2024, and it is likely that we could see good performance among the recent price war started by BYD. The valuation of NIO is quite modest when we compare with other peers in China. Even a few percentage point improvement in margins over the next few quarters along with good delivery numbers deliver a good bullish sentiment towards the stock.
